_readme: All user-facing prose for Footnotes to Plato. Edit the string values; do NOT rename or remove keys — the views look them up by name. Keys marked _html accept inline tags (<b>, <i>, <em>); all others are plain text and any tags will show literally. Placeholders in {braces} are filled in by the page and must be kept exactly as written.

view_a.page_title: Footnotes to Plato — which works get attention
view_a.heading: Which works get attention
view_a.subtitle: Citation volume across the ancient-philosophy scholarship, work by work
view_a.intro: Plot how attention to any work or group moved across the journals from 1887 to 2022. Add works to compare them, or switch to the field view to see where philosophy and classics journals diverge. Lines count citations we could place with confidence; a solid line resolves reliably, a dashed one under 80% — so read its shape rather than its height.

legend.fade: detected but unplaceable — could be much more

trustworthy.heading: Trustworthy
trustworthy.note_html: At least 80% of these works' citations resolve to a specific passage. Bar lengths are reliable — you can compare how much these works are studied. Two cautions: works marked small n clear the threshold on very little evidence, and works marked uneven coverage have a specific stretch of pages that resolves worse than the rest, so their shape is less even than the bar suggests.

uncertain.heading: Uncertain
uncertain.note_html: Under 80% resolve. The solid floors are real lower bounds, but the long fades show how much citation traffic couldn't be attributed. A short floor here does not mean a work is little-studied — it means much of its attention is unplaceable. Don't rank these by floor alone, and don't compare their floors against the trustworthy tier.

view_a.footnote: On author colouring: works are grouped by the reference edition their citations use — Stephanus pagination (indigo) or Bekker pagination (teal) — which is how the scholarship addresses them, not a position on authorship. Several works in each corpus are of disputed or rejected authenticity; they are coloured by the edition that paginates them, as traditionally ascribed, and no claim about who wrote them is intended.

smalln.note_html: Works marked small n have fewer than {threshold} placed citations. Their resolution rate is computed from so little evidence that it says more about chance than about how well the work resolves — a work with 30 citations can reach 100% by luck. Tier membership here is not a claim that the work is well studied, and these bars should not be compared with the long ones.

bands.tooltip: under-resolved pages {ranges} — roughly {missing} citations there could not be placed
bands.legend: a stretch of pages resolving worse than the rest of the work

view_b.heading: Passage-level attention
view_b.subtitle: Which passages of a work the scholarship actually cites

trustworthy.label: Trustworthy tier · publication grade
trustworthy.body_html: At least 80% of this work's detected citations resolve to a specific passage. The profile below is a reliable picture of where attention falls: you can compare section heights within this work and cite them with confidence.

uncertain.label: Uncertain tier · read with care
uncertain.body_html: Fewer than 80% of this work's detected citations could be pinned to a specific passage — its page-numbers collide with other works, so much of its citation traffic is unplaceable. What you see is a floor, not the full picture. A short bar here means "we couldn't place these," not "few cite this." Trust the shape of what resolved, and don't read the absolute heights as complete or compare them against trustworthy-tier works.

drill.empty: No citing articles found for this passage.

errors.load_failed: Could not load the citation data for this work. Reload the page; if it persists the data file may be missing.

bands.banner_html: A few page-ranges resolve worse than the rest of this work (marked above): {ranges}. An estimated {missing} citations there could not be placed, so read those stretches as a floor. Full detail on the methods page.
